tspan.tspan: Department of Physics, California Institute of Technology, Pasadena, California
91107 Received May 7, 1981 1. INTRODUCTION On the program it says this is a keynote speech--and I don't know what a keynote speech i
I do not intend in any way to suggest what should be in this meeting as a keynote of the subjects or anything like that. I have my o
things to say and to talk about and there's no implication that anybody needs to talk about the same thing or anything like it. So w
I want to talk about is what Mike Dertouzos suggested that nobody would talk about. I want to talk about the problem of simulating p
with computers and I mean that in a specific way which I am going to explain. The reason for doing this is something that I learned
from Ed Fredkin, and my entire interest in the subject has been inspired by him. It has to do with learning something about the poss
of computers, and also something about possibilities in physics. If we suppose that we know all the physical laws perfectly, of cour
we don't have to pay any attention to computers. It's interesting anyway to entertain oneself with the idea that we've got something
to learn about physical laws; and if I take a relaxed view here (after all I'm here and not at home) I'll admit that we don't unders
everything. The first question is, What kind of computer are we going to use to simulate physics? Computer theory has been developed
to a point where it realizes that it doesn't make any difference; when you get to a universal computer, it doesn't matter how it's m
how it's actually made. Therefore my question is, Can physics be simulated by a universal computer? I would like to have the element
of this computer locally interconnected, and therefore sort of think about cellular automata as an example (but I don't want to forc
it). But I do want something involved with the locality of interaction. I would not like to think of a very enormous computer with a
interconnections throughout the entire thing. Simulating Physics with Computers Richard P. Feynman Department of Physics, California
Institute of Technology, Pasadena, California 91107 Received May 7, 1981 1. INTRODUCTION On the program it says this is a keynote sp

same thing or anything like it. So what I want to talk about is what Mike Dertouzos suggested that nobody would talk about. I want t

of computers, and also something about possibilities in physics. If we suppose that we know all the physical laws perfectly, of cour
we don't have to pay any attention to computers. It's interesting anyway to entertain oneself with the idea that we've got somethin
to learn about physical laws; and if I take a relaxed view here (after all I'm here and not at home) I'll admit that we don't unders
everything. The first question is, What kind of computer are we going to use to simulate physics? Computer theory has been developed
to a point where it realizes that it doesn't make any difference; when you get to a universal computer, it doesn't matter how it's
how it's actually made. Therefore my question is, Can physics be simulated by a universal computer? I would like to have the eleme
of this computer locally interconnected, and therefore sort of think about cellular automata as an example (but I don't want to for
it). But I do want something involved with the locality of interaction. I would not like to think of a very enormous computer with a
interconnections throughout the entire thing. Simulating Physics with Computers Richard P. Feynman Department of Physics, Californi

I don't know what a keynote speech is. I do not intend in any way to suggest what should be in this meeting as a keynote of the subj
or anything like that. I have my own things to say and to talk about and there's no implication that anybody needs to talk about the
same thing or anything like it. So what I want to talk about is what Mike Dertouzos suggested that nobody would talk about. I want t
talk about the problem of simulating physics with computers and I mean that in a specific way which I am going to explain. The reaso

to a point where it realizes that it doesn't make any difference; when you get to a universal computer, it doesn't matter how it's m
how it's actually made. Therefore my question is, Can physics be simulated by a universal computer? I would like to have the element
of this computer locally interconnected, and therefore sort of think about cellular automata as an example (but I don't want to forc
it). But I do want something involved with the locality of interaction. I would not like to think of a very enormous computer with a
interconnections throughout the entire thing. Simulating Physics with Computers Richard P. Feynman Department of Physics, California
Institute of Technology, Pasadena, California 91107 Received May 7, 1981 1. INTRODUCTION On the program it says this is a keynote sp
I don't know what a keynote speech is. I do not intend in any way to suggest what should be in this meeting as a keynote of the subj
or anything like that. I have my own things to say and to talk about and there's no implication that anybody needs to talk about the
same thing or anything like it. So what I want to talk about is what Mike Dertouzos suggested that nobody would talk about. I want t
talk about the problem of simulating physics with computers and I mean that in a specific way which I am going to explain. The reaso
Department of Physics, California Institute of Technology, Pasadena, California

we don't have to pay any attention to computers. It's interesting anyway to entertain oneself with the idea that we've got something
to learn about physical laws; and if I take a relaxed view here (after all I'm here and not at home) I'll admit that we don't unders
everything. The first question is, What kind of computer are we going to use to simulate physics? Computer theory has been developed
to a point where it realizes that it doesn't make any difference; when you get to a universal computer, it doesn't matter how it's m
how it's actually made. Therefore my question is, Can physics be simulated by a universal computer? I would like to have the element
of this computer locally interconnected, and therefore sort of think about cellular automata as an example (but I don't want to forc
it). But I do want something involved with the locality of interaction. I would not like to think of a very enormous computer with a
interconnections throughout the entire thing. Simulating Physics with Computers Richard P. Feynman Department of Physics, California
Institute of Technology, Pasadena, California 91107 Received May 7, 1981 1. INTRODUCTION On the program it says this is a keynote sp
I don't know what a keynote speech is. I do not intend in any way to suggest what should be in this meeting as a keynote of the subj
or anything like that. I have my own things to say and to talk about and there's no implication that anybody needs to talk about the
same thing or anything like it. So what I want to talk about is what Mike Dertouzos suggested that nobody would talk about. I want t
talk about the problem of simulating physics with computers and I mean that in a specific way which I am going to explain. The reaso
Department of Physics, California Institute of Technology, Pasadena, California
